title: Electricity Is Just Water in Pipes

questions.q: In the water analogy, what does VOLTAGE represent?
options: The pressure pushing the water
The amount of water flowing
The size of the pipe
The complete loop of pipe
questions.why: Voltage is the pressure that pushes the water; the amount actually flowing is current (amps).

questions.q: In the water analogy, what does CURRENT (amps) represent?
options: How much water is actually flowing
The pressure pushing the water
Whether the loop is open or closed
The color of the pipe
questions.why: Current (amps) is how MUCH water is actually flowing; voltage is the pressure that pushes it.

questions.q: You connect a 9-volt battery but leave a gap in the wire so the loop is not complete. Does any current flow?
options: No — nothing flows until the loop is closed
Yes — the battery forces current across the gap
Yes, but only half as much
Only if you raise the voltage higher
questions.why: Just like no water comes out until the pipe goes all the way around, nothing flows until the loop is a complete, closed circuit.

questions.q: Which comparison is correct about pressure versus a wall outlet?
options: A 9-volt battery is a small pump; a wall outlet is like high city water pressure
A 9-volt battery has more pressure than a wall outlet
Both supply exactly the same pressure
Neither one supplies any pressure
questions.why: A 9V battery is a small pump (low pressure) while a wall outlet is like strong city water pressure (much higher voltage).

questions.q: On a 24VDC blender control panel, you measure 24.2V at the power supply terminals with the circuit open, and you sign it off as good. Later a relay coil on that circuit burns out after only two months. What's the most likely explanation, based on how this kind of failure actually happens?
options: DC coils don't fail from voltage problems, so something else like vibration caused it
The coil must have been defective from the manufacturer, since the supply voltage was confirmed correct
The open-circuit reading at the supply didn't prove the coil was actually getting full voltage under load — a bad connection somewhere in the path was dropping voltage only when current flowed
24.2V is over the rated voltage for a 24VDC coil, so it was overvoltage from the start
questions.why: An open-circuit (no-load) reading only proves the source is fine — it says nothing about what the coil actually sees once current flows through the real wiring. A corroded or under-torqued connection can be invisible with no load but drop many volts once the coil demands current, slow-cooking it exactly like the Line 3 war story. 24.2V is within the 85%-110% window, so it isn't overvoltage, and blaming the coil or vibration skips the step every tech should do first: measure at the coil terminals under load.

questions.q: You're walking a dead control circuit with a meter, measuring hot-to-neutral across each wire segment from the power supply toward the load. At one segment the reading jumps from near-zero to full source voltage. What does that jump tell you?
options: That segment has a short circuit and needs to be replaced immediately
You just crossed an open — a break, blown fuse, or failed terminal — right at that segment
You've found a properly working section of the circuit and should keep walking past it
The meter is picking up capacitive coupling and the reading should be ignored
questions.why: Voltage appears across an open because the source is pushing on both sides of the gap with nothing completing the loop between them — that jump from near-zero to full voltage IS the signature of an open, whether it's a broken wire, blown fuse, or failed terminal. A short circuit would instead read near-zero everywhere since there's no meaningful resistance to drop voltage across. Capacitive coupling causes small floating readings on a dead run measured to ground, not a full-voltage jump measured properly across the gap.

questions.q: A 24VDC control relay coil is spec'd at 240 ohms. During a PM, you pull the coil out of circuit and measure 178 ohms cold with an ohmmeter. What should you do?
options: Increase the supply voltage to compensate so the coil pulls in the same current as spec
Ignore it — coil resistance always drifts with age and has no bearing on reliability
Install it anyway, since lower resistance just means it will draw less current and run cooler
Treat it as suspect and replace it — a resistance well below spec on a coil usually means partial insulation breakdown from past overvoltage or overheating
questions.why: Coil resistance dropping well below the datasheet number is exactly what the module flags as a sign the coil has already been partially cooked by overvoltage or a bad supply — replace it rather than trust it back into service. Lower resistance means MORE current draws (I = V/R), not less, which runs it hotter, not cooler; and 'never bears on reliability' ignores a documented failure pattern. Raising supply voltage to force spec current is backwards and would push it further into overvoltage.

questions.q: A 24VDC coil draws 0.1A sealed-in through a wire run and terminal splice with a combined 40 ohms of resistance. Using Ohm's Law, how many volts get lost in that path before reaching the coil, and does the coil still fall within its safe 85%-110% operating window?
options: No voltage is lost in a DC circuit unless the coil is also drawing inrush current
0.4 volts are lost, leaving the coil essentially at full 24V with plenty of margin
4 volts are lost (0.1A x 40Ω), leaving 20V at the coil — right at the edge of the 85% floor (20.4V), so it's marginal and worth investigating
40 volts are lost, since resistance in ohms converts directly to volts lost regardless of current
questions.why: V = I x R gives 0.1A x 40Ω = 4V dropped in the bad path, leaving only 20V at the coil out of a 24V supply — that's below the 85% floor (20.4V) laid out in the Field Notes, so this coil is right at the point of unreliable pull-in or chatter. Volts lost always requires multiplying by current, not reading the ohms directly; and DC circuits absolutely drop voltage across resistance any time current flows, inrush or not.

questions.q: A tech decides it's fine to leave an oxidized terminal splice in a 24VDC control circuit in place because 'it still works, just a little warm.' What's the real danger of ignoring rising resistance at a connection like this?
options: Oxidized connections self-clean over time as current flows through them, so it will resolve on its own
The main risk is just nuisance relay chatter, which is annoying but not a safety concern
Nothing — resistance in a splice only affects current, never heat, so it's a cosmetic issue at most

Heat from P = I² x R grows with the square of current, so as load increases even slightly, that connection can go from warm to a fire risk while also starving the coil of enough voltage to operate reliably
questions.why: Because heat is P = I² x R, doubling the current through that bad joint doesn't double the heat, it quadruples it — a splice that ran warm for years can become a real fire risk the moment load increases, and the same resistance is simultaneously stealing voltage the coil needs to pull in reliably. This isn't cosmetic, and it isn't just chatter — it's a heat and fire hazard on top of a reliability problem. Oxidized contacts get worse under current and heat cycling, they don't self-clean.

questions.q: You're troubleshooting a circuit that has run fine for years, but ever since a second load was added to the same branch, a terminal block downstream has started running noticeably hot. What's the most likely explanation using what you know about resistance and heat?
options: The terminal is fine — all terminals get warm when a circuit has been running for years, regardless of current
The added load increased total current through that terminal, and since heat follows P = I² x R, even a small pre-existing resistance at that terminal now dissipates much more heat than before
The extra load lowered the circuit's voltage, and lower voltage always causes terminals to overheat
Adding a second load can never affect an unrelated terminal's temperature, so the terminal must have failed coincidentally at the same time
questions.why: This is precisely the scenario the module calls out: a marginal connection with some resistance can run cool for years, but P = I² x R means heat scales with the SQUARE of current, so adding a second load and increasing current can suddenly turn a fine connection into a hot one. It's not coincidence, and terminals don't just get warm from age alone — rising heat at a specific point means rising current or rising resistance there. Lower voltage isn't what's driving the heat; current through resistance is.
